next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By the time they reached Salt Room the river had stopped smelling like river. The mud dropped out of it mile by mile. What came up instead was iron, kelp, old shells crushed to chalk, and the cold sour breath of sea caves. Nami stood in the bow of Sereya's old funeral ferry with spray drying on her lips and tried not to take the taste as an answer. The ruined chapel sat ahead on a black tooth of stone where the last cut met the Gray Sea, its roof punched inward, its walls furred white with salt. Every few breaths the tide shoved through whatever hollowness lived under it and the whole place exhaled.

In the stitched pocket at her hip, the salt-fig seed she had kept all this time clicked softly against her bone.

She had told herself she carried it for proof. Then she had told herself she carried it because throwing it away felt unlucky. Then because she had gone so long with it that getting rid of it would make the first nights real again: the drowned girl on the ferry-house boards, the seed shining wet as an eye. Here, with the sea opening its rotten mouth under the chapel, Nami stopped lying to herself about it. She had carried it because some ugly hot part of her wanted one thing in this whole flood to belong to her alone.

Riva drove the ferry with a borrowed Halcote pole and the old scar on her chin gone pale with strain. She had been sleeping in bites for days. Every command came out flat now, stripped of anything but use.

"Iven, get the bow line ready. Lale, watch the left wall. Nami, stay where the boat knows your weight."

"The boat doesn't know anyb...
open_hooks_to_carry: The siblings split their labor for the first time, with Lale leading inland containment.
Families resist admitting their own kin are entranced until they see it firsthand.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ven, who steadied the hull from the mud. Riva lifted under her knees and shoulders. Lale kept the bandaged hand from knocking the doorframe. Daro held the blanket around her so the morning cold wouldn't get in at the neck. It was awkward, unceremonious, and so careful it hurt.

The ferry rocked when Riva stepped in. Water slapped the flat hull. Up close, the brass rings smelled green and old. Somebody, years ago, had scratched a little fish into the inside rail with a nail tip; Nami ran her thumb over it before she sat. She did not remember doing that as a child, but the kind of person who scratched a fish into a funeral ferry felt like one of them.

Iven shoved them off the mud with both hands and jumped in after, landing light despite the exhaustion. Riva took the stern pole. Lale spread the ledger and her tattooed arm side by side under the waxed cloth. Daro stood at the bow listening, head tipped, not to the dead alone but to the ordinary trouble of current against reeds.

The hut receded at once, small and warped and smelling, a place built for wayfarers nobody expected to stay. The ferry moved slower than the Bitter Thread had ever moved and closer to danger, so low to the water that Nami could see each swirl take shape before it touched them. Nothing about it felt triumphant. They had traded engine for muscle, gear for hands, illusion for a blunt count of what each body could still do.

No one said they would manage. No one said they were ready.

Riva planted the pole, leaned her weight into it, and the ferry slid into the reed lane.

This time, nobody stayed behind.
state_deltas_required: Sleepwalkers in Halcote, split responsibilities, and the need to win practical trust from inland towns.
Lale proves she can lead outside the family, and inland communities begin treating the siblings as necessary rather than cursed.
